messages.content:
which journal could we submit for 根据电子数据分析子宫内膜癌，the following is the paper draft. see it and give some potential journals we can submit and i will check their latex templates. ACTL8 Functions as a Prognostic-Related Biomarker, and Its High Expression Correlates with Tumor Immune Infiltration and UCEC Progression
Xiaoyue Gao, Jialian Mao, Huaijun Zhou, Lu Xu
Abstract
Background:
Methods:
Results:
Keywords:
Introdction
Materials and Methods
2.1 ACTL8 mRNA and protein expression differences between Endometrial Cancer and normal tissues
In our study, we investigated the levels of ACTL8 mRNA expression in tissue samples of endometrial cancer and endometrial hyperplasia using a UCEC dataset (GSE106191) that was retrieved from the gene expression omnibus (GEO) database (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/). The ggplot2 package [3.3.6] was utilized for data visualization, while the stats package [4.2.1] and car package [3.1-0] were employed for statistical analysis. The protein levels of ACTL8 in UCEC were then determined using the university of alabama at birmingham cancer data analysis portal (UALCAN) database (http://ualcan.path.uab.edu/), an internet application that allows users to analyze the cancer genome atlas (TCGA) and gene transcription data online. Statistics were considered significant when p < 0.05.
2.2 Relationships between ACTL8 Expression and a Variety of Clinical Traits in UCEC
Firstly, we utilized the “Clinical” and “Subtype” modules of TISIDB (an integrated repository portal for tumor-immune system interactions) website (http://cis.hku.hk/TISIDB/) to observe the correlations between ACTL8 expression level and cancer stages, tumor grades, molecular subtypes, and immune subtypes of UCEC. Then, the Xiantao Academic website (https://www.xiantao.love/) provided box plots for ACTL8 expression levels of individuals with various clinical features in UCEC. The RNA-seq data and linked clinical data were retrieved from the TCGA database (https://portal.gdc.cancer.gov/) in level 3 HTSeqfragments per kilobase per million (FPKM) format, translated to transcripts per million reads (TPM) format, and analyzed after log2 conversion. For statistical analysis and data visualization, the ggplot2 package [3.3.6] was utilized, along with the stats package [4.2.1] and car package [3.1.0]. To detect two groups of data, the Shapiro-Wilk normality test, Kruskal-Wallis test, and Multiple hypothesis test (Dunn’s test) with Bonferroni’s method to adjust significant level were employed. Ultimatly, in order to verify and strengthen the previous findings, we uesd the UALCAN database to analyze the connection between ACTL8 gene expression and clinicopathological characteristics in UCEC. A statistically significant p-value was defined as one less than 0.05.
2.3 Survival Prognosis Analysis in Different UCEC Clinical Subgroups
First, we evaluated the diagnostic ability of ACTL8 for UCEC utilizing the receiver operating characteristic curve
(ROC curve) of the Xiantao Academic web. Utilizing the pROC program [1.18.0], the data underwent ROC analysis, and ggplot2 [3.3.6] was utilized to display the outcomes. Next, we investigated how ACTL8 affected the OS, relapse free survival (RFS), DSS, and PFI of UCEC using onlinetools like Kaplan-Meier Plotter (http://kmplot.com/analysis/)  and Xiantao Academic. We further looked into the relationships between ACTL8 expression and prognosis (OS, DSS, and PFI) in distinct UCEC clinical subgroups using Kaplan-Meier plots on the Xiantao Academic website. In this website, the median ACTL8 expression was used as a cutoff value to classify groups (low expression group: 0–50%; high expression group: 50–100%). For the proportional hazards assumption test and fitting survival regression, the Survival package [3.3.1] was utilized. The ggplot2 and survminer programs [3.3.6] provided visual representations of the findings. In the hypothesis test, the log-rank test and cox regression were adopted. p value less than 0.05 means statistical significance.
2.4 ACTL8 Coexpression Network Analyse in UCEC
we used two of three analytical modules in the LinkedOmics database (http://linkedomics.org/login.php) :
LinkFinder and LinkInterpreter. We utilized LinkFinder to find the ACTL8 coexpressed genes using the Pearson correlation test, and the findings were shown using a volcano plot, heat maps, and scatter diagrams. Subsequently,
utilizing gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA), we applied LinkInterpreter to the GO_BP (gene ontology biological process) and KEGG (kyoto encyclopedia of genes and genomes) pathways of ACTL8 and its coexpressed genes. As statistically significant, a p-value of less than 0.05 was considered.
Then, we opened the web page (https://cn.string-db.org/) of STRING (search tool for the retrieval of interacting genes/proteins) to get ACTL8 binding proteins that have been experimentally verified and we calculated the intersection of the two sets of data—ACTL8 binding proteins and ACTL8-coexpressed genes—using Draw Venn Diagram (https://bioinformatics.psb.ugent.be/webtools/Venn/). To make the results more convincing, we used the function of “Single gene coexpression heat map” in Xiantao Academic to verify the correlation between ACTL8 and the genes in the cell cycle pathway network obtained from PathCards website (https://pathcards.genecards.org/). Coexpression heat maps were produced using the ggplot2 package [3.3.6] to illustrate the analysis findings (ns, p ≥ 0.05; _, p< 0.05;_ *, p < 0.01; ***, p < 0.001).
2.5 DNA Methylation Status in the CpG Islands of the ACTL8 Gene and Their Prognostic Value in UCEC
DNA hypermethylation at promoters can result in gene silence. To further pinpoint the mechanisms underlying the upregulation of ACTL8 in UCEC, the methylation levels of ACTL8 in the UCEC dataset were analyzed via the UALCAN database. In addition, we also compared the differential expression of ACTL8 and its promoter methylation status in UCEC patients with various tumor grades (grades 1, 2, 3, and 4). Not only does DNA methylation during carcinogenesis affect gene expression, but also the prognosis of cancer patients. MethSurv
(https://biit.cs.ut.ee/methsurv/) is a website that uses TCGA data to perform survival analysis based on DNA methylation biomarkers. DNA methylation status of the ACTL8 gene’s CpG sites and the prognostic significance of these CpG sites in UCEC were explored in the UCEC–TCGA dataset using the MethSurv database. p value less
than 0.05 means statistical significance.
2.6 Associations of ACTL8 Expression and Its Methylation Status with Immune Cells, Immunomodulators, and
Chemokines in UCEC
In our work, the associations between ACTL8 expression and its methylation status and immune cells, immunomodulators, and chemokines were evaluated by the Xiantao Academic and TISIDB websites. The ggplot2 package [3.3.6] was used to illustrate the analysis findings, which was carried out with R programming language [4.2.1].
2.7 Statistical Analysis
Examining the ACTL8 expression differences between normal (or hyperplasia) and malignant tissues was done utilizing the Wilcoxon rank sum test (Mann-Whitney U test), Paired Samples T test, and Independent Sample T test. The Kruskal-Wallis test, Dunn’s test, Wilcoxon rank sum test, Welch one-way ANOVA test, Games-Howell test, and Independent-Sample T Test were utilized to explore the association between ACTL8 expression and clinicopathological features. The area under the curve (AUC) acquired from the time-dependent ROC curve analysis was employed to assess the ACTL8’s UCEC diagnosis accuracy. Additionally, utilizing the Kaplan-Meier (KM) curve, which employs the Log-rank test and Cox regression, the potential predictive significance of ACTL8 expression in UCEC patients with different clinical pathological features was evaluated. Pearson Correlation Analysis was utilized to search for gene sets that correlated positively and negatively with ACTL8 expression in UCEC and conduct gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) analysis. The expression association between ACTL8 and genes relevant to the cell cycle pathway network as well as immunological checkpoints in UCEC was examined using Spearman association Analysis. The influence of each ACTL8 gene DNA methylation site’s methylation status on the UCEC patients’ prognosis was examined using the likelihood ratio test. To investigate the relationship between ACTL8 expression and the enrichment of 24 different types of immune cells in UCEC TME, Spearman Correlation Analysis was employed. To compare the immune cell enrichment scores of the high and low ACTL8 expression groups, the Wilcoxon rank sum test was employed. At p < 0.05, statistical significance was established.
Results
3.1 ACTL8 was Upregulated in UCEC
We discovered that ACTL8 was highly expressed in UCEC (Fig. 1A,B). We also verified that ACTL8’s expression was considerably higher in UCEC than in matched nearby normal tissues (Fig. 1C). Additionally, the GSE 106191 dataset’s endometrial cancer tissues showed greater levels of ACTL8 expression than the endometrial hyperplasia tissues (p＜0.05) (Fig. 1D). Results from the UALCAN database further indicated that ACTL8’s mRNA and protein expression was increased in endometrial cancer tissues (Fig. 1E-H).
3.2 ACTL8 was Linked to a Variety of Clinical Features in UCEC
We used Xiantao Academic to present the relationship between ACTL8 and various clinical features in UCEC, and discovered that ACTL8 was expressed at a greater level in patients with stage III, IV, age＞60, histological type (serous), histologic grade (G2, G3) and tumor invasion ≥50% (Fig. 2A-L, respectively). Then, using the UALCAN database, we looked at ACTL8 expression in UCEC with various clinical features and discovered that ACTL8 expression was substantially varied in weight, tumor grade, and tumor history (Fig. 3A-M, respectively).
3.3 ACTL8 Prognostic Value in UCEC
The area under the ROC curve was 0.62, demonstrating ACLT8’s accuracy in predicting UCEC and its positive diagnostic impact on this condition (Fig. 4A). Additionally, we discovered that the OS, RFS, DSS, and PFI of UCEC patients with high ACTL8 expression were worse (Fig. 4B-F). So we then looked at the relationships between ACTL8 and prognosis (OS, DSS, and PFI) in several UCEC clinical subgroups. Increased ACLT8 expression was associated with a lower OS in most clinical subgroups, including subgroup of race (white), clinical stage (stage III), Primary therapy outcome (CR), age (≤60, >60), body mass index (BMI) >30, Histological type (Endometrioid), Residual tumor (R0), Histologic grade (G3), Tumor invasion (<50%, ≥50%), Menopause status (post), Diabetes (No, Yes), Hormones therapy (No), Radiation therapy (No, Yes), Weight >80kg, Height (≤160cm, >160cm) (Fig. 5A-O2, respectively). For DSS, increased ACTL8 expression was associated with a lower DSS in subgroup of race (white), clinical stage (stage I, III), Primary therapy outcome (CR), age (≤60, >60), body mass index (BMI) >30, Histological type (Endometrioid), Residual tumor (R0), Histologic grade (G3), Tumor invasion (<50%, ≥50%), Menopause status (post), Diabetes (No, Yes), Hormones therapy (No), Radiation therapy (No, Yes), Weight (>80kg), Height (≤160cm, >160cm) (Fig. 6A-O2, respectively). For PFI, increased ACTL8 expression was associated with a lower PFI in subgroup of race (white), clinical stage (stage I, III), Primary therapy outcome (CR), age (≤60, >60), body mass index (BMI) >30, Histological type (Endometrioid), Residual tumor (R0), Histologic grade (G2, G3), Tumor invasion (<50%, ≥50%), Menopause status (post), Diabetes (No, Yes), Hormones therapy (No), Radiation therapy (No), Weight (>80kg), Height (≤160cm, >160cm) (Fig. 7A-O2, respectively).
3.4 ACTL8 Coexpression Network Correlated with the
In UCEC, 10511 genes (dark red dots) were substantially positively connected to ACLT8, whereas 9387 genes (dark green dots) were inversely proportional to ACTL8 (FDR, 1.00E-11) (Fig. 8A). In addition, Supplementary Table 1 included a list of all ACTL8 coexpressed genes. The top 50 genes that were positively and negatively linked with ACTL8 were exhibited on heat maps (Fig. 9A, C). The top 12 genes that were positively and negatively linked with ACTL8 were exhibited on scatter diagrams (Fig. 9B, D). GAB4 (GRB2 associated binding protein family member 4), TPX2 (TPX2 microtubule nucleation factor), TTX (tetrodotoxin-sensitive), FAM46C (family with sequence similarity 46 member C), TRIP13 (thyroid hormone receptor interactor 13), and DUSP9 (dual specificity phosphatase 9) had the strongest association with ACTL8 expression (r = 0.543394, 0.492184, 0.487052, -0.48633, 0.479784, 0.478287, p = 6.57E-15, 3.99E-12, 7.16E-12, 7.77E-12, 1.61E-11, 1.90E-11, and FDR = 6.54E-11, 2.64E-08, 3.09E-08, 3.09E-08, 5.35E-08, 5.40E-08, respectively).
The GO_BP and KEGG analysis of ACTL8 coexpressed genes were then determined using GSEA. We looked into the GO biological process categories and discovered that ACTL8 and its coexpressed genes were mostly involved in . Then, we carried out KEGG pathway analysis, which revealed that ACTL8 coexpressed genes were prominent in (Fig. 10 and Supplementary Table 2).
Discussion
Conclusions
Availability of Data and Materials
Author Contributions
Ethics Approval and Consent to Participate
Acknowledgment
Funding
This study was funded by (grant number )
Conflict of Interest
The authors declare no conflict of interest.
References
Fig.1. Actin-like 8(ACTL8) mRNA and protein expression in uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC). (A–C) ACTL8 mRNA expression levels in endometrial cancer patients and matched adjacent normal samples. (D) ACTL8 mRNA expression levels were upregulated in the endometrial cancer tissues compared to endometrial hyperplasia tissues in the GSE106191 dataset.
(E) ACTL8 mRNA expression levels in uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma (UCEC) based on the university of alabama at birmingham cancer data analysis portal (UALCAN). (F) ACTL8 mRNA expression levels in individual cancer stages in UCEC based on UALCAN. (G) ACTL8 protein expression levels in UCEC based on UALCAN. (F) ACTL8 protein expression levels in individual cancer stages in UCEC based on UALCAN. _p < 0.05;_ **, p <0.001;** **, p < 0.0001.
Fig.2. Relationships between ACTL8 expression and several clinical features in UCEC. (A) Clinical stage; (B) Race; (C) Age; (D) BMI; (E) Histological type; (F) Histologic grade; (G) Tumor invasion (%); (H) Diabetes; (I) Hormones therapy; (J) Radiation therapy; (K) Residual tumor; (L) Primary therapy outcome. _p < 0.05;_ *p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001. PD, progressive disease; SD, stable disease; PR, partial response; CR, complete response.
Fig. 3. Associations between ACTL8 protein expression(A-G) and mRNA expression(H-M) anddifferent clinical characteristics in UCEC analyzed by the UALCAN database. (A) Patient’s race; (B) Patient’s age; (C) Patient’s weight; (D) Tumor grade; (E)Tumor history; (F) p53
; (G)Chromatin modifier altered; (H) Patient’s race; (I) Patient’s age; (J) Patient’s weight; (K) Menopause status; (L) Histological subtypes; (M) TP53 muation status. _p < 0.05;_ *p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001. TCGA, the cancer genome atlas.
Fig. 4. ACTL8’s prognostic value in UCEC. (A) The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve reflected the ACTL8’s diagnostic ability for UCEC; (B,C) Kaplan-Meier (KM) plots showing ACTL8’s influence on poorer overall survival (OS) (B) and recurrence free survival (RFS) (C) in UCEC patients from the Kaplan-Meier Plotter website; (D–F) KM plots showing ACTL8’s influence on OS (D), disease-specific survival (DSS) (E), and progress free interval (PFI) (F) in UCEC patients from the Xiantao Academic website. All p values were less than 0.05. FPR, false positive rate.
Fig. 5. Relationships between the OS and ACTL8 expression in various UCEC clinical subgroups. (A) Race: White; (B) Clinical stage: Stage III; (C) Primary therapy outcome: CR; (D1) Age: <=60; (D2) Age: >60; (E) body mass index (BMI) >30; (F) Histological type: Endometrioid; (G) Residual tumor: no residual tumor (R0) (H) Histologic grade: G3; (I1) Tumor invasion (%): <50; (I2) Tumor invasion (%): >=50; (J) Menopause status: Post; (K1) Diabetes: No; (K2) Diabetes: Yes;  (L) Hormones therapy: No; (M1) Radiation therapy: No; (M2) Radiation therapy: Yes; (N) Weight >80; (O1) Height: <=160; (O2) Height: >160. All p values were less than 0.05.
Fig. 6. Associations between ACTL8 expression and the DSS in different clinical subgroups of UCEC. (A) Race: White; (B1) Clinical stage: Stage I; (B2) Clinical stage: Stage III; (C) Primary therapy outcome: CR; (D1) Age: <=60; (D2) Age: >60; (E) body mass index (BMI) >30; (F) Histological type: Endometrioid; (G) Residual tumor: no residual tumor (R0); (H) Histologic grade: G3; (I1) Tumor invasion (%): <50; (I2) Tumor invasion (%): >=50; (J) Menopause status: Post; (K1) Diabetes: No; (K2) Diabetes: Yes;  (L) Hormones therapy: No; (M1) Radiation therapy: No; (M2) Radiation therapy: Yes; (N) Weight >80; (O1) Height: <=160; (O2) Height: >160. All p values were less than 0.05
Fig. 7. Associations between ACTL8 expression and the PFI in different clinical subgroups of UCEC. (A) Race: White; (B1) Clinical stage: Stage I; (B2) Clinical stage: Stage III; (C) Primary therapy outcome: CR; (D1) Age: <=60; (D2) Age: >60; (E) body mass index (BMI) >30; (F) Histological type: Endometrioid; (G) Residual tumor: no residual tumor (R0); (H1) Histologic grade: G2; (H2) Histologic grade: G3; (I1) Tumor invasion (%): <50; (I2) Tumor invasion (%): >=50; (J) Menopause status: Post; (K1) Diabetes: No; (K2) Diabetes: Yes;  (L) Hormones therapy: No; (M) Radiation therapy: No; (N) Weight >80; (O1) Height: <=160; (O2) Height: >160. All p values were less than 0.05.
Fig. 8. ACTL8’s co-expressed genes and interacting proteins. (A) Volcano plot obtained from the LinkedOmics database showing ACTL8’s co-expressed genes tested by Pearson test in UCEC cohort; (B) Protein-protein interaction network of ACTL8 obtained from the STRING online tool; (C) Venn diagram showed the intersection result of ACTL8’s co-expressed genes and interacting proteins.
Fig. 9. ACTL8’s positively and negatively co-expressed genes in UCEC analyzed by the Linked Omics database. (A, C) the top 50 negatively co-expressed (A) and positively co-expressed (C) genes of ACTL8 shown in heat maps; (B, D) the top 12 negatively co-expressed (B) and positively co-expressed (D) genes of ACTL8 represented by scatter plots. A p value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.
Fig. 10. ACTL8’s gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) analysis in UCEC cohort analyzed by the Linked Omics database. (A) Bar chart of ACTL8’s gene ontology biological process (GO_BP) (biological process) analysis; (B) Bar chart of ACTL8’s gene ontology cellular component (GO_CC) (cellular component) analysis; (C) Bar chart of ACTL8’s gene ontology molecular function (GO_MF) (molecular function) analysis; (D) Bar chart of ACTL8’s Kyoto encyclopedia of genes and genomes (KEGG) pathways. (E) Each Biological Process, Cellular Component and Molecular Function category is represented by a red, blue and green bar, respectively. The height of the bar represents the number of IDs in the user list and also in the category. A false discovery rate (FDR) value of 0.05 or less was considered statistically significant.
Fig. 11. The heat maps obtained from the Xiantao Academic web showing the correlation between ACTL8 and the genes in the cell cycle pathway network queried from the PathCards website in UCEC. ns, not significant. _, p < 0.05;_ *, p < 0.01; ***, p < 0.001
Fig.12. DNA methylation levels in the ACTL8 gene were associated with the prognosis of UCEC patients. (A) The heat map of DNA methylation at CpG sites in the ACTL8 gene by the MethSurv database. (B–C) The association between methylation levels of two CpG islands of the ACTL8 gene and OS of UCEC patients: (B) cg03220543, and (C) cg09821328.
Fig. 13. Correlation analysis of ACTL8’s expression and immune cells’ infiltration level as well as immune checkpoints in UCEC conducted in the Xiantao Academic online tool. (A–T) The correlation between ACTL8’s expression and infiltration level of immune cells. (U) Expression correlation analysis of ACTL8 and immune checkpoint-related genes. ns, not significant. _p < 0.05;_ *p < 0.01; ***, p < 0.001

Your draft is a **TCGA/GEO-based bioinformatics study** in **UCEC (endometrial cancer)** focusing on **a single-gene biomarker (ACTL8)** with **expression + prognosis + methylation + immune infiltration + pathway/coexpression** analyses. Journals that commonly publish this *exact* style of paper (and usually provide LaTeX templates) fall into: (1) gynecologic oncology, (2) cancer biomarkers/translational, (3) bioinformatics/omics, (4) immuno-oncology (bioinformatic).

Below are realistic targets to check (I’m not judging novelty/acceptance probability—just topical fit).

## A. Gynecologic / women’s cancer–focused (best topical match)
1. **BMC Cancer** (Springer Nature) – broad oncology; many TCGA biomarker papers  
2. **Journal of Ovarian Research** (BMC/Springer Nature) – often accepts uterine/gynecologic oncology bioinformatics too  
3. **Cancer Cell International** (BMC/Springer Nature) – frequent TCGA+immune infiltration biomarker articles  
4. **Gynecologic Oncology Reports** (Elsevier) – more clinical/translation oriented; check if purely in-silico is acceptable  
5. **International Journal of Gynecological Cancer** (BMJ) – higher clinical bar; may require stronger validation

## B. Biomarkers / translational oncology (very common for single-gene prognostic papers)
6. **Biomarkers** (Taylor & Francis)  
7. **Cancer Biomarkers** (IOS Press)  
8. **Biomolecules** (MDPI) – many bioinformatics biomarker+immune papers  
9. **Diagnostics** (MDPI) – if you emphasize diagnostic ROC + methylation + immune context  
10. **Genes** (MDPI) – gene-focused mechanistic/bioinformatic analyses

## C. Omics / bioinformatics-oriented (fit if you position as integrative analysis)
11. **BMC Genomics** (Springer Nature) – needs a strong “genomics/analysis” framing rather than only one gene  
12. **BMC Medical Genomics** (Springer Nature) – similar, more clinical-genomics angle  
13. **Frontiers in Genetics** / **Frontiers in Oncology** – has sections that routinely publish TCGA immune-infiltration biomarker papers  
14. **PLOS ONE** – broad, methodologically-sound bioinformatics studies (novelty threshold lower than specialty journals)

## D. Tumor immunology / microenvironment angle (if you strengthen immune component)
15. **Cancer Immunology, Immunotherapy** (Springer) – higher bar; usually needs more validation  
16. **Frontiers in Immunology** – if immune infiltration/checkpoint findings are central and well-developed

---

### Quick fit notes (based on your current draft)
- Your study is **entirely in-silico** (TCGA/GEO + web tools: UALCAN, TISIDB, LinkedOmics, MethSurv, STRING, KMplot). Journals in **BMC / MDPI / Frontiers / PLOS ONE** are typically the most compatible.
- **AUC=0.62** is modest; journals that emphasize “diagnostic biomarker” might ask for stronger performance or validation.
- Many journals will ask for **external validation** (another GEO cohort), or **wet-lab validation** (IHC/qPCR) if aiming higher-tier gynecologic oncology journals.

---

### To narrow to 3–5 best options, tell me:
1) Do you need **SCI/Q1/Q2**, or any indexed journal is fine?  
2) Do you want **Open Access only**? (BMC/MDPI/Frontiers are OA; some others hybrid)  
3) Any **APC budget limit**?  
4) Do you have (or can add) **independent validation cohort** or **IHC**?

If you answer these, I can rank the most suitable journals and also point you to their **official LaTeX template locations**.
1.  sci is fine. the higher index is better, but consider that we would better to make it accept in 8 months. 2. open or close are either ok. 3. no. 4. currently no.

Given (i) SCI preferred, (ii) you want a realistic **≤8 months to acceptance**, (iii) **no external cohort / no wet-lab validation**, the safest strategy is to target journals that **routinely accept TCGA-only (plus webtool) biomarker + immune infiltration papers** and have **fast editorial cycles**. Higher-tier gynecologic oncology journals often request experimental/independent validation and can be slower.

## Recommended journal targets (good fit + typically faster)
### Tier 1 (best balance of SCI + speed + acceptance likelihood without wet-lab)
1) **BMC Cancer** (Springer Nature)  
- Fit: very common venue for TCGA prognostic/immune infiltration biomarker papers  
- Speed: generally reasonable; APC (OA) usually required  
- Risk: moderate revision requests but usually not strict on wet-lab if analysis is solid

2) **Cancer Cell International** (Springer Nature / BMC)  
- Fit: extremely common for “single gene + TCGA + immune infiltration + methylation + GSEA” style papers  
- Speed: often relatively fast  
- Risk: may ask for one extra validation (e.g., another GEO), but not always

3) **Frontiers in Oncology** (Frontiers)  
- Fit: many UCEC/TCGA immune infiltration biomarker studies  
- Speed: can be fast to first decision; acceptance within months is feasible if reviews go smoothly  
- Risk: reviewer variability; they may request extra analyses but rarely require wet-lab

4) **PLOS ONE** (SCI, broad)  
- Fit: accepts computational-only studies if methods are correct and conclusions are appropriately limited  
- Speed: often predictable; good “backup” option  
- Risk: may require strong statistical rigor and clarity, but not novelty

### Tier 2 (often fast, SCI, but more “template-like” papers—needs careful writing to avoid desk rejection)
5) **Biomolecules (MDPI)**  
- Fit: frequent TCGA biomarker + immune infiltration papers  
- Speed: very fast in many cases  
- Risk: desk rejection possible if novelty seems limited; must present clean narrative + limitations

6) **Diagnostics (MDPI)**  
- Fit: if you emphasize diagnostic/prognostic utility + ROC + methylation biomarkers  
- Speed: fast  
- Risk: your AUC=0.62 is modest, so avoid overselling “diagnostic biomarker”

7) **Genes (MDPI)**  
- Fit: gene-centered integrative analyses  
- Speed: fast  
- Risk: same novelty concerns as above

## Higher impact but higher risk (without validation)
These are “try if you’re willing to gamble on revision burden/time”:
- **Journal of Translational Medicine** (BMC) – good journal, but reviewers often want stronger validation.  
- **Clinical Epigenetics** (Springer Nature) – could match methylation angle, but usually expects deeper epigenetic work.  
- **International Journal of Gynecological Cancer** / **Gynecologic Oncology Reports** – more clinical; may want clinical/experimental validation.

---

## Practical submission plan to maximize acceptance within ~8 months
**Plan A (most realistic):**  
- Submit to **Cancer Cell International** *or* **BMC Cancer** first.

**Plan B (if you want speed):**  
- **Frontiers in Oncology** *or* **Biomolecules/Diagnostics**.

**Plan C (reliable backup):**  
- **PLOS ONE**.

This “A→B→C” sequence gives a good chance of landing acceptance within your timeline.

---

## One key warning: TCGA-only + many webtools = common reviewer criticisms
To reduce major revision cycles (which break the 8‑month goal), consider adding *at least one* low-effort strengthening step (no wet lab needed):
- **External validation of expression/prognosis** in 1–2 GEO cohorts (if available for UCEC) or another public dataset.
- **Multivariate Cox regression** including stage/grade/age (many reviewers insist on this).
- **Use consistent immune infiltration method** (e.g., CIBERSORT/xCell/TIMER) and report which; web portals sometimes obscure details.
- **Correct multiple testing (FDR)** wherever appropriate and state it clearly.

Even adding just **one external dataset validation** often dramatically increases acceptance probability in SCI journals.

---

## LaTeX templates (so you can quickly check)
- **BMC journals (BMC Cancer, Cancer Cell International):** Springer Nature “BMC” LaTeX template is standard.  
- **Frontiers:** Frontiers LaTeX template (they provide Overleaf too).  
- **PLOS ONE:** PLOS LaTeX template available.  
- **MDPI (Biomolecules/Diagnostics/Genes):** MDPI LaTeX template is standard across MDPI.

If you tell me which 3–5 journals you prefer from the list above, I can:
1) rank them specifically for your manuscript style, and  
2) give the exact template links / Overleaf template names, and  
3) suggest small edits to align with that journal’s typical structure (e.g., how to present “web-tool” analyses to avoid desk rejection).

turn the following descriptions into latex format: **Associations between ACTL8 expression and the DSS in different clinical subgroups of UCEC.** (A) Race: White; (B1) Clinical stage: Stage I; (B2) Clinical stage: Stage III; (C) Primary therapy outcome: CR; (D1) Age: <=60; (D2) Age: >60; (E) body mass index (BMI) >30; (F) Histological type: Endometrioid; (G) Residual tumor: no residual tumor (R0); (H) Histologic grade: G3; (I1) Tumor invasion (%): <50; (I2) Tumor invasion (%): >=50; (J) Menopause status: Post; (K1) Diabetes: No; (K2) Diabetes: Yes;  (L) Hormones therapy: No; (M1) Radiation therapy: No; (M2) Radiation therapy: Yes; (N) Weight >80; (O1) Height: <=160; (O2) Height: >160. All _p_ values were less than 0.05

turn the following descriptions into latex format: **ACTL8’s co-expressed genes and interacting proteins.** (A) Volcano plot obtained from the LinkedOmics database showing ACTL8’s co-expressed genes tested by Pearson test in UCEC cohort; (B) Protein-protein interaction network of ACTL8 obtained from the STRING online tool; (C) Venn diagram showed the intersection result of ACTL8’s co-expressed genes and interacting proteins.

turn the following descriptions into latex format: **ACTL8’s positively and negatively co-expressed genes in UCEC analyzed by the Linked Omics database.** (A, C) the top 50 negatively co-expressed (A) and positively co-expressed (C) genes of ACTL8 shown in heat maps; (B, D) the top 12 negatively co-expressed (B) and positively co-expressed (D) genes of ACTL8 represented by scatter plots. A _p_ value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

turn the following descriptions into latex format:**ACTL8’s gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) analysis in UCEC cohort analyzed by the Linked Omics database.** (A) Bar chart of ACTL8’s gene ontology biological process (GO_BP) (biological process) analysis; (B) Bar chart of ACTL8’s gene ontology cellular component (GO_CC) (cellular component) analysis; (C) Bar chart of ACTL8’s gene ontology molecular function (GO_MF) (molecular function) analysis; (D) Bar chart of ACTL8’s Kyoto encyclopedia of genes and genomes (KEGG) pathways. (E) Each Biological Process, Cellular Component and Molecular Function category is represented by a red, blue and green bar, respectively. The height of the bar represents the number of IDs in the user list and also in the category. A false discovery rate (FDR) value of 0.05 or less was considered statistically significant.

turn the following descriptions into latex format:**The heat maps obtained from the Xiantao Academic web showing the correlation between ACTL8 and the genes in the cell cycle pathway network queried from the PathCards website in UCEC.** ns, not significant. _, p < 0.05;_ *, _p_ < 0.01; ***, _p_ < 0.001

```latex
\textbf{Heat maps showing correlations between ACTL8 and genes in the cell-cycle pathway network in UCEC.}
Heat maps obtained from the Xiantao Academic web show the correlations between ACTL8 expression and genes in the cell-cycle pathway network queried from the PathCards website.
ns, not significant; $p \geq 0.05$; $^{*}p < 0.05$; $^{**}p < 0.01$; $^{***}p < 0.001$.
```

turn the following descriptions into latex format: **DNA methylation levels in the ACTL8 gene were associated with the prognosis of UCEC patients.** (A) The heat map of DNA methylation at CpG sites in the ACTL8 gene by the MethSurv database. (B–C) The association between methylation levels of two CpG islands of the ACTL8 gene and OS of UCEC patients: (B) cg03220543, and (C) cg09821328.

```latex
\textbf{DNA methylation levels in the ACTL8 gene are associated with the prognosis of UCEC patients.}
(A) Heat map of DNA methylation at CpG sites in the ACTL8 gene generated using the MethSurv database.
(B--C) Associations between methylation levels of two CpG sites within the ACTL8 gene and overall survival (OS) in UCEC patients:
(B) cg03220543; (C) cg09821328.
```

turn the following descriptions into latex format: **Correlation analysis of ACTL8’s expression and immune cells’ infiltration level as well as immune checkpoints in UCEC conducted in the Xiantao Academic online tool.** (A–T) The correlation between ACTL8’s expression and infiltration level of immune cells. (U) Expression correlation analysis of ACTL8 and immune checkpoint-related genes. ns, not significant. _p < 0.05;_ *_p_ < 0.01; ***, _p_ < 0.001

```latex
\textbf{Correlation analysis of ACTL8 expression with immune cell infiltration and immune checkpoints in UCEC conducted using the Xiantao Academic online tool.}
(A--T) Correlations between ACTL8 expression and the infiltration levels of immune cells.
(U) Expression correlation analysis between ACTL8 and immune checkpoint-related genes.
ns, not significant; $p \geq 0.05$; $^{*}p < 0.05$; $^{**}p < 0.01$; $^{***}p < 0.001$.
```
